content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 17, 2009

John Brown
Best Best & Krieger
One Corporate Plaza
3500 Porsche Way, Suite 200
Ontario, CA 91764

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our file No. A-09-278

Dear Mr. Brown:

     This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
Additionally, our advice is limited to obligations arising under the Act. We do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.

QUESTION

     Even though the Directors of the Hi-Desert Water District have disqualifying conflicts of interest, are any of them able to participate in the governmental decisions described below based on the "Public Generally" or "Legally Required" exceptions to the prohibition?

CONCLUSION

     The "public generally" and "legally required" exceptions do appear to apply to the Directors' situations and would allow each to vote, despite a disqualifying conflict of interest.

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-09-278
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are General Counsel for the Hi-Desert Water District (the "District") and you
request advice on behalf of elected Directors Sheldon Hough, Sarann Graham, Roger Mayer, and
Dan Munsey (collectively, the "Directors"). The Directors will be participating in decisions
related to a proposed construction of a regional wastewater collection, treatment, and disposal
system (the "Project") that will be on-going and accomplished in three phases. The Project will
serve residential, commercial, and industrial customers and will aim to accomplish the following:
1) reduce and/or replace the need of existing sewer septic systems, 2) improve the collection and
treatment of wastewater, and 3) reduce the amount of nitrate contamination affecting the Warren
Valley Groundwater Basin.

The District will attempt to cover costs through bonds, grants, low-interest loans or other
alternatives. There will likely be costs to the public, however, including contributions by
property owners with improved real property in the District, future connection fees, and
contributions through existing ratepayers in the District. The rate structure is currently under
review.

The first phase will involve constructing a wastewater reclamation facility designed for
an initial one million gallons per day treatment capacity, developing a sewage collection system
to serve the developed area of Yucca Valley, constructing percolation basins for the treated
effluent to return the effluent to the Warren Valley Groundwater Basin. Approximately 50
percent of the District's water customers will be able to connect to the Project at the completion
of the first phase.

You have stated that each director has the economic interests in the District described
below:

Hough:
•     Business entity: dentistry practice.
•     Real property: building/property for dentistry practice.
•     Source of income: dentistry patients (to the extent that he received $500 or
more in the last 12 months of any one patient).
•     Personal finances: a public official always has an interest in his or her
personal finances.

Graham
•     Real property: personal residence and second lot on the adjoining property
containing a garage.
•     Personal finances: a public official always has an interest in his or her
personal finances.

Mayes
•     Real property: personal residence, commercial lot.

File No. A-09-278
Page No. 3

•     Source of income: rent income from business that lease space on the
commercial lot.
•     Personal finances: a public official always has an interest in his or her
personal finances.

Munsey

•     Business entity: Real estate business.
•     Source of income: Regulation 18703.3(c)(2)(C) defines the sources of
income to a real estate agent or brokerage for whom the agent works, the client represented by the agent and any person who receives a finder's or referral fee for referring the sale to the agent. A person who purchases or sells the real property from which the agent receives his or her fee is not a source of income to the agent unless it is the agent's client.
•     Personal finances: a public official always has an interest in his or her
personal finances.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, which we apply to your question. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)

Steps One and Two: Are the Directors Public Officials and Are They Making, Participating in Making, or Influencing Governmental Decisions?

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act only apply to public officials. (Section 87100.) Public officials include members of committees, boards or commissions with decision-making authority. (Regulation 18701(a)(1).) Officials of all special purpose districts are also included under the provisions of the rule. As a member of the Hi-Desert Water District, the Directors are public officials under the Act.

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the scope of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) You have stated that the directors will be voting on the Project, and are thus making governmental decisions.

File No. A-09-278
Page No. 4

Step Three: What Are the Directors' Applicable Economic Interests?

Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any of the official's economic interests. The applicable economic
interests include:

1. An interest in a business entity in which a public official has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a), Regulation
18703.1(a).) An interest in any business entity in which a public official is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management. (Section 87103(d), Regulation 18703.1(b).)
2. An interest in real property in which a public official has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b), Regulation 18703.2.)
3. Any source of income, including promised income, to the public official that
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87103(c), Regulation 18703.3.)
4. Any source of gifts to the public official if the gifts aggregate to $420 or more
within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e), Regulation 18703.4.)
5. A public official also has an economic interest in his or her personal
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate
family. This is also known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section
87103, Regulation 18703.5.)

As detailed above, the Directors have interests in their income, businesses, and real
properties, as well as their personal finances.

To recap, Director Hough has economic interests in his business entity (dentistry
practice), real property (the property on which the dentistry practice sits), his patients as sources
of income, if he received more than $500 in the past 12 months from any one patient, and his
personal finances.

Director Graham has an economic interest in her personal residence, a double lot with the
second lot containing a garage as well as her personal finances.

Director Mayes has an economic interest in the real property on which his personal
residence sits as well as a commercial lot he owns. He has a source of income economic interest
in the rent payments he receives from the businesses that lease space on that commercial lot. He
also has an interest in his personal finances.

Director Munsey has an economic interest in his real estate business as a business entity.
It is unclear what his role is in this business, whether he also acts as an agent, and whether this is

2 A public official has an interest in his or her real property, provided that interest is worth $2,000 or more.
We assume in all instances above that this is the case.

File No. A-09-278
Page No. 5

a brokerage business. The source of income to a real estate agent is the brokerage for whom the agent works, the client he or she represents, and any person who receives a finder's fee for referring the sale.

The Directors have an economic interest in their personal finances and those of their immediate families. As stated in Regulation 18703.5, a governmental decision will have a personal financial effect "if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family increasing or decreasing."

Steps Four through Six: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decisions will have a material financial effect on the economic interests?

You have conceded that the governmental decisions involved will materially affect the Directors' economic interests. Each Director has real property that will experience a change in value and each Director's personal finances will also be affected. Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will have a material effect on each of the other economic interests does not affect this analysis, as seen in Step Seven, below.

Step Seven: Public Generally

Each of the Directors has a conflict of interest in making governmental decisions related to the Project. Notwithstanding a determination that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material, a public official does not have a disqualifying conflict of interest in the governmental decision if the official can establish that the governmental decision will affect the public official's economic interests in a manner which is indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally. (Regulation 18707.)

You explained that the District will be imposing taxes or fees on the properties within its jurisdiction to complete the Project. There are special rules that apply to rates, assessments, and other similar decisions. The financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally in certain instances, including when the decision is made by the governing board of a water, irrigation, or similar district to establish or adjust assessments, taxes, fees, charges, or rates, among other decisions. The amounts must be applied proportionally on the officials' interests as well as at least ten percent of the property owners or those receiving services from the agency. (Regulation 18707.2(c).)

You have explained that the District will be assessing whether to institute fees or taxes in addition to other sources of income for the Project. Provided these fees are applied on a proportional, "across the board" basis and are also imposed upon at least ten percent of the jurisdiction, the "public generally" exception applies. You have stated that the fees will be assessed on a proportional basis and will affect about 50 percent of the properties within the District, bringing the decisions squarely within this exception.

File No. A-09-278
Page No. 6


Step Eight: Legally Required Participation

While it appears that the "public generally exception" applies, as explained above, there
is another exception that might apply to the Directors as well. Section 87101 provides a
mechanism through which a decision-making body can avoid paralysis when it is impossible to
assemble a quorum of members who are not disqualified from participating in a decision by
Section 87100, the Act's general rule governing conflicts of interest. Section 87101 provides as
follows:

"Section 87100 does not prevent any public official from making or participating
in the making of a governmental decision to the extent his participation is legally
required for the action or decision to be made. The fact that an official's vote is
needed to break a tie does not make his participation legally required for purposes
of this section."

Regulation 18708 elaborates the process by which Section 87101 is given effect. In brief,
when a decision-making body cannot muster a quorum of members free from any conflict of
interest, one or more of the disqualified officials – the number to make up a bare quorum – are
selected to take part in the decision at issue, and in any ancillary decisions where the conflict
persists. (See generally, Battersby Advice Letter, No. I-02-141.)

Your current request for advice and our prior letter written to the fifth (of five) Director
(Stadium Advice Letter, A-07-012) concedes that each Director has a disqualifying conflict of
interest. If, after applying the above "Public Generally Exception" a quorum of Directors has
conflicts of interest the "Legally Required Exception" might apply.

With respect to the method to be employed for selecting which disqualified
councilmember is "legally required" to participate in a decision, we have advised that a random
means of selection must be used to select only the number of officials needed. (Tiffany Advice
Letter, No. I-08-007.) Also, all disqualified Directors must participate in the selection process.
(See Steele Advice Letter, No. A-05-071.)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division

HMR:jgl
